summary:
• Colombia's 2026 election presents critical supply risk as the country's oil industry faces potential policy shifts that could impact Western Hemisphere production levels.

• Maritime chokepoint vulnerabilities are escalating, with multiple ship attacks in the Strait of Hormuz and geopolitical tensions threatening crude transit routes that affect global oil pricing and regional supply stability.

• Weakening crude demand from China—the world's largest importer—combined with U.S. production expansion is creating downward price pressure that could constrain profitability and capital investment across the Americas upstream sector.

articles.title: China’s Oil Buying Pause Won’t Last Forever
articles.description: China has been cited as an example of a country that has managed to insulate itself relatively well against oil crises. articles.title: Cuba Bets on Solar Power as Energy Crisis Deepens
articles.description: Cuba has been experiencing a worsening energy crisis for several years, which previously led it to rely heavily on Venezuela for its fuel. articles.title: Russia Signs Military Cooperation Deal With Afghanistan’s Taliban Government

articles.title: Morocco Is Emerging as a Renewable Energy Superpower
articles.description: Morocco is rapidly becoming a renewable energy powerhouse thanks to its favourable weather conditions and proximity to Europe. The North African country has rapidly developed its solar energy sector and is now looking to become a major green hydrogen and sustainable shipping hub.

articles.title: Kretinsky Sees Potential to Boost TotalEnergies Stake
articles.description: Czech billionaire Daniel Kretinsky said he's open to increasing his holding in TotalEnergies SE, after gaining a stake in the French oil major last month.

articles.description: Oil sank to a six-week low as the US and Iran tentatively agreed to extend a ceasefire, stoking optimism that the Strait of Hormuz may soon reopen.
